km.England: ប្រទេសអង់គ្លេស, uណៃteត គីnហ្គដុម

km.Lancashire, England: ឡាncaស៊ីរe, ប្រទេសអង់គ្លេស, uណៃteត គីnហ្គដុម

zh.Amounderness, Lancashire, England: 英國英格蘭藍卡爾阿姆德內斯

zh.Shrewsbury, Shropshire, England: 英國史洛普爾史福斯波利
zh.lifeSummary: 當Sir John De La Lee於1195年出生在英國史洛普爾時，他的父親Reginald De La Lee33歲，母親Reyna de Lega33歲。他於1221年與Rei M. Lega結婚。他們至少是1個兒子的父母。他去世於1301年在英國英格蘭享年106歲。

zh-hans.Amounderness, Lancashire, England: 英国英格兰藍卡爾阿姆德內斯
zh-hans.Alderton Hall, Shrewsbury, Shropshire, E: 英国史洛普爾史福斯波利
zh-hans.Shrewsbury, Shropshire, England: 英国史洛普爾史福斯波利
zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Sir John De La Lee于1195年出生在英国史洛普爾时，他的父亲Reginald De La Lee33岁，母亲Reyna de Lega33岁。他于1221年与Rei M. Lega结婚。他们至少是1个儿子的父母。他去世于1301年在英国英格兰享年106岁。

nameMeanings.name: Sir
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: German: variant of   Sier  , unexplained. This surname is very rare in Germany.
Czech (Šír): nickname from Middle High German schīr ‘sheer, pure, clean’. Compare Schier .

variants.name: Sier
meanings: Dutch and German: from a short form of the personal name Sierik, Sierich, composed of the ancient Germanic elements sigi ‘victory’ + rīc ‘power(ful)’, or from a short form of the personal name Sigihard, composed of the ancient Germanic elements sigi + hari ‘army’.
English: variant of   Syer  .

nameMeanings.name: John
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English and Welsh: ultimately from the Hebrew personal name Yoḥanan ‘Jehovah has favored (me with a son)’ or ‘may Jehovah favor (this child)’. This personal name was adopted into Latin (via Greek) as Johannes, and has enjoyed enormous popularity in Europe throughout the Christian era, being given in honor of Saint John the Baptist, precursor of Christ, and of Saint John the Evangelist, author of the fourth gospel, as well as the nearly one thousand other Christian saints who bore the name. Some of the principal forms of the personal name in other languages are: Welsh Ieuan, Evan, Siôn, and Ioan; Scottish Ia(i)n; Irish Séan; German Johann, Johannes; Dutch and Slavic Jan; French Jean; Italian Giovanni; Spanish Juan; Portuguese João; Greek Iōannēs (vernacular Giannis, Yannis); Russian Ivan. There were a number of different forms of the name in Middle English, including Jan(e), a male name (see Jayne ); Jen (see Jenkin ); Jon(e) (see Jones ); and Han(n) (see Hann ). By the beginning of the 14th century John rivalled William in popularity and has always been a favorite name. Johan became Jo(h)n, and another Old French form Jehan was shortened to Jan and Jen, giving rise to Old French and Middle English diminutives such as Jonin, Janin, and Jenin. More common in Middle English were Jankin, Jonkin, and Jenkin, which were Middle Dutch pet forms introduced after the Conquest by Flemish and Picard settlers. The most common pet form of John was Jack, another borrowing from Flemish and Picard usage. Han may sometimes have been a short form of Johan but was more usually a pet form of Henry. There were also various Middle English feminine versions of this name (e.g. Joan, Jehan), some of which were indistinguishable from masculine forms. The distinction on grounds of gender between John and Joan was not firmly established in English until the 17th century. It was even later that Jean and Jane were specialized as specifically female names in English; bearers of these surnames and their derivatives are more likely to derive them from a male ancestor than a female. As a surname in the British Isles, John is particularly frequent in Wales, where it is a late formation representing Welsh Siôn rather than the older form Ieuan (which gave rise to the surname Evan ). In North America, the English form of the surname has absorbed many cognates from other languages, e.g. Assyrian/Chaldean Youkhana , French Jean , Hungarian János (see Janos ), Slovenian Janež and Janeš (see Janes ), Czech Jan , Albanian Gjoni , and their derivatives (see examples at Johnson ). The name John is also found among Christians in southern India (compare Ninan and Yohannan ), but since South Indians traditionally do not have hereditary surnames, the southern Indian name was in most cases registered as such only after immigration of its bearers to the US.
German: from a North German and Silesian variant of the personal name   Johannes  . This surname is also found in France (Alsace and Lorraine). Compare   Yohn  .

variants.name: Ancar
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Francois, Alphonse, Etienne, Fabien, Mireille.

Probably an altered form of French (Pas-de-Calais, Nord) Ancart, itself a variant of Hancart and Hanecart: from a pet form (double diminutive) of the personal name Han, a short form of French Jehan and Flemish Johan(nes) (see John ).

variants.name: Bongiovanni
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Mario, Antonio, Dante, Salvatore, Santo, Antonino, Domenic, Gaetano, Geno, Giacomo, Gilda, Luciano, Nino.
Italian: from a personal name composed of the elements bon ‘good’ + Giovanni, an equivalent of John .

variants.name: Di Giovanna
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Gaspar, Alfonso, Carlo, Domenic, Gaetano, Liborio, Nunzio, Pietro, Rosario, Sal, Salvatore.
Italian: metronymic from the female personal name Giovanna, an equivalent of Joan (see John ).

variants.name: Anes
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Angel, Dionisio, Esperanza, Gilberto, Ines, Juanita, Luis, Luisa, Manuel, Manuela, Pedro.  Portuguese Joao.
Portuguese and Galician: patronymic from a shortened form of the Latin personal name Johannes (Portuguese João, Galician Xoán; see John ).

variants.name: Burhans
meanings: German: from Middle Low German būr ‘farmer, peasant’ + the personal name Hans, a short form of Johannes (see John ), hence a distinguishing occupational name ‘farmer John’ with Hans being a very frequent name. See also Burian .

variants.name: Breihan
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Erwin, Kurt.
German: distinguishing nickname for a (beer) brewer, from Middle High German briuw(er) ‘brew(er)’ + a short form of the personal name Johann(es) (see John ).

variants.name: Jayne
meanings: English (Gloucestershire) and Welsh (Monmouthshire): from the Middle English male personal name Jan(e), from Old French Jehan, a variant of Johan, which gave rise to the common alternative John . Sometimes the name may also arise from the Welsh variant Ieun of Welsh Ieuan, likewise equivalent to John (compare Evan ).

variants.name: Di Giovanni
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Angelo, Salvatore, Vito, Rocco, Sal, Aldo, Carlo, Gino, Giuseppe, Guido, Agostino, Carmela.
Italian: patronymic from the personal name Giovanni, an equivalent of John . Compare De Giovanni , Dejohn , and Giovanni .

variants.name: Eanes
meanings: English (Hampshire): probably a variant of Heanes with loss of initial H-, itself a variant of Heane, with post-medieval excrescent -s, a nickname from Middle English hen(e), heine ‘poor, needy, wretched’ (Old English hēan). Eanes is now rare in Britain, and in the US is found predominantly in VA.
Portuguese and Galician: patronymic from the Latin personal name Johannes (Portuguese João, Galician Xoán; see John ).

variants.name: Ene
meanings: Romanian: from a derivative of the personal name Ioan (see John ).
West African (Nigeria): unexplained.

nameMeanings.name: De
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.
Indian: variant of   Dey  .

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: La
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Vietnamese Hung, Cuong, Anh, Thanh, Ha, Hoang, Tuan, Chau, Hao, Hien, Hoa, Hue, Nam, Phong, Sinh, Tuong, Min, Tam, Tanh, Thai, Tham, Tinh, Yeong.  Chinese Hong, Hang, Sang, Tong, Chan, Chi, Hwa Ja, Hyung, Kam, Ki Bong, Kin, Man.
Vietnamese: from the Chinese surname 羅, see   Luo   1.
Vietnamese (Lã): from the Chinese surname 呂, see Lu 6 and 3 below.

variants.name: Luo
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Wei, Jian, Yi, Ping, Yong, Zhen, Feng, Hong, Ming, Jin, Bin, Chen, Hai, Lan, Min, Huan, Hung, Shen, Tian.
Chinese:

Mandarin form of the surname 羅, meaning ‘net for catching birds’ or ‘to collect’ in Chinese: (i) from Luo (羅), the name of an ancient state (located in present-day Hubei province). After it was annexed by the state of Chu during the early Spring and Autumn period (770–476  BC ), people from the state migrated southwards and adopted Luo (羅) as their surname. (ii) from the Po Duo Luo (破多羅) and Chi Luo (叱羅) families from the Xianbei ethnic group in northern China, who changed their original surnames to 羅 during the Northern Wei dynasty (386–534  AD ). (iii) from the personal name Hu Se Luo (斛瑟羅), leader of an ancient Turkic tribe who are believed to have migrated to China during the Tang dynasty (618–907  AD ). His descendants inherited his name Hu Se Luo (斛瑟羅) as their surname and shortened it to 羅 during the Northern Wei dynasty (386–534  AD ). (iv) from the name of one of the seven families from the Bandun Man, an ethnic group residing in ancient southwestern China (located mainly in present-day Sichuan province).

nameMeanings.name: Lee
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Young, Sang, Jae, Jong, Jung, Sung, Yong, Kyung, Seung, Dong, Kwang, Myung.

English: topographic name for someone who lived near a meadow or a patch of arable land, Middle English lee, lea, from Old English lēa, dative case (used after a preposition) of lēah, which originally meant ‘wood or glade’.

English: habitational name from any of the many places in England named with Old English lēah ‘wood, glade’, including Lee in Buckinghamshire, Essex, Hampshire, Kent, and Shropshire, and Lea in Cheshire, Derbyshire, Herefordshire, Lancashire, Lincolnshire, and Wiltshire.

variants.name: Ley
meanings: English: variant of   Lee  .
German: from a medieval personal name, Eloy, from Latin Eligius (see Eligio ), a Christian saint's name.
German (Rhineland): topographic name from Middle High German leie ‘rock, stone, slate’, or a habitational name from any of several places called with this word. Compare Leier .

variants.name: Lees
meanings: English and Scottish: topographic name from Middle English lees ‘fields, arable land’, plural of lee (see Lee ), or from Middle English lese ‘pasture, meadow’ (Old English lǣs).

English: habitational name fromLeece or Lees in Lancashire, or Leese in Cheshire, all named from OldEnglish lēas ‘woodland clearings’ (plural oflēah), or from Leece in Cumbria, which was probably namedwith a Celtic word, lïss ‘hall, court, the principalhouse in a district’.
English: variant of   Leece   1.

variants.name: Lay
meanings: English: variant of   Lee  .
Scottish: shortened form of   McClay  .
German: habitational name from any of the places so named, in the Rhineland near Koblenz and in Bavaria, from lay(h), a word meaning ‘stone, rock, slate’.

variants.name: Laye
meanings: English: variant of   Lee  .
Chinese: variant Romanization of the surname 李, based on its Cantonese pronunciation. See   Li   1.

variants.name: Lie
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Scandinavian Erik, Oystein, Sven, Thor.  German Hans, Gunther, Hilde.  Korean Han, Chang Ho, Chen, Chun, Hok, Jung, Kam, Liang, Lie, Ling, Shuen, Yung.
Norwegian: variant of Li 11, a habitational name from any of numerous farmsteads named Li, from Old Norse hlíth ‘mountain slope, hillside’. In Norway this is the most frequent spelling.
German: unexplained.

variants.name: Layman
meanings: English: topographic name for a dweller by a woodland clearing or a meadow, from Middle English ley + man. Compare Lee .
English: nickname from layman, a word denoting an uneducated person.
Americanized form of German   Lehmann  .

variants.name: Lea
meanings: English: variant of   Lee  .
Chinese: variant Romanization of the surnames 李 and 利, see   Li   1 and 3.

variants.name: Lye
meanings: English: variant of   Lee   1, a topographic name for someone who lived near a patch of arable land, or a habitational name from Lye in Herefordshire (with the same etymology).

Norwegian: habitational name from a farmstead in Rogaland named Lye, Old Norse Lýgi meaning ‘alliance, covenant’, used to denote a place sanctified by such an agreement, such as a court or council meeting place.
Altered form of German   Ley  .

variants.name: Saelee
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Southeast Asian Chan, Kao, Nai, Chai, Cheng, Chien, Foo, Hin, Muang, Pao, Seng, Song, Sou.
Southeast Asian (Iu Mien): from the name of the Lee clan of the Iu Mien people from Laos + the Thai prefix sae, denoting a clan. Compare Lee 8.

variants.name: Leigh
meanings: English: habitational name from any of numerous places (in at least sixteen counties, but especially Leigh in Lancashire) named either with the nominative case of Old English lēah ‘woodland clearing’ (see Lee ) or with lēage, a late dative form of this word (see Lye ).
Chinese: variant Romanization of the surname 李, based on its Cantonese pronunciation. See   Li   1.

historicEvents.title: Magna Carta
historicEvents.description: King John of England agreed to a charter of rights called the Magna Carta. The document was originally drafted by the Archbishop of Canterbury to establish peace between King John and a group of rebel barons. The Magna Carta was completed in 1215 and is now considered to be one of the greatest constitutional documents of all time.

historicEvents.title: France Declares War on England
historicEvents.description: France declared war on England as Louis VIII of France attacked Poitou and northern Gascony.

historicEvents.title: 100 Years of Conflict Between England and France Ends
historicEvents.description: Treaty of Paris was signed on December 4, 1259, which ended 100 years of conflict between England and France.

historicEvents.title: Jews Expelled From England
historicEvents.description: After years of anti-Semitic behavior, King Edward I expelled Jews from England in 1290.

en.lifeSummary: When Sir John De La Lee was born in 1195, in Shropshire, England, his father, Reginald De La Lee, was 33 and his mother, Reyna de Lega, was 33. He married Rei M. Lega in 1221. They were the parents of at least 1 son. He died in 1301, in England, United Kingdom, at the age of 106.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Sir John De La Lee est né en 1195, à Shropshire, Angleterre, son père, Reginald De La Lee, avait 33 ans et sa mère, Reyna de Lega, avait 33 ans. Il a épousé Rei M. Lega en 1221. Ils ont eu au moins 1 fils. Il est décédé en 1301, à Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, à l’âge de 106 ans.

de.lifeSummary: Als Sir John De La Lee 1195 in Shropshire, England geboren wurde, war sein Vater Reginald De La Lee 33 und seine Mutter Reyna de Lega 33. Er hat 1221 Rei M. Lega geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 1 Sohn. Er starb 1301 im Alter von 106 Jahren in England, United Kingdom.

it.lifeSummary: Quando Sir John De La Lee è nato il nel 1195 nel Shropshire, England, il suo padre, Reginald De La Lee, aveva 33 anni e il suo madre, Reyna de Lega, aveva 33 anni. Lui sposato Rei M. Lega nel 1221. Erano genitori di almeno 1 figlio. Morì nel 1301, nel Inghilterra, Regno Unito, all'età di 106 anni.

ja.lifeSummary: Sir John De La Leeが1195年にイギリスシュロプシュアイアで生まれた時，父Reginald De La Leeは33歳，母Reyna de Legaは33歳であった。1221年にRei M. Legaと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも1人の息子の親であった。彼は1301年にイギリスイギリスで106歳で死去した。

ko.lifeSummary: Sir John De La Lee(이)가 1195년 영국 슈럽실르에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Reginald De La Lee(은)는 33세였고, 그의 어머니 Reyna de Lega(은)는 33세였다. 그는 1221년 Rei M. Lega(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 한 명의 아들을 두었다. 그는 1301년 영국 잉글랜드에서 106세의 나이로 사망했다.

pt.lifeSummary: Quando Sir John De La Lee nasceu em 1195, em Shropshire, Inglaterra, seu pai, Reginald De La Lee, tinha 33 anos e sua mãe, Reyna de Lega, tinha 33 anos. Ele casou-se com Rei M. Lega em 1221. Eles tiveram pelo menos 1 filho. Ele faleceu em 1301, em Inglaterra, United Kingdom, com 106 anos.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Sir John De La Lee родился в 1195 г. в местности шропшир, Англия, его отцу по имени Reginald De La Lee было 33 года, а матери, которую звали Reyna de Lega, 33. Он женился на женщине по имени Rei M. Lega в 1221 г. У них был по крайней мере 1 сын. Он умер в 1301 г. в местности Англия, Великобритания в возрасте 106 лет.

es.lifeSummary: Cuando Sir John De La Lee nació en 1195, en Shropshire, Inglaterra, su papá, Reginald De La Lee, tenía 33 años y su mamá, Reyna de Lega, tenía 33 años. Se casó con Rei M. Lega en 1221. Fueron padres de por lo menos 1 hijo. Murió en 1301, en Inglaterra, Reino Unido, a la edad de 106 años.
